revision.comment: [[物部氏]] へのリダイレクトを削除しました
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|歴史用語|日本の古代氏族|物部氏|兵庫県津名郡にあった村|物部村 (兵庫県)|四国放送の女性アナウンサー|物部純子|その他の用法|物部 (曖昧さ回避)|}} '''物部'''（もののべ／もののふ）とは、古代[[日本]]の朝廷の兵（軍事）・刑（刑罰）に携わった[[部民]]。令制諸官司にも配属されている[[品部]]。
== 概要 == 氏族的系譜を有し、[[大化の改新]]以前は[[物部氏|物部連]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきた。その数が多かったので、物部の八十氏などと呼ばれた。
『日本書紀』によると、崇神天皇7年の11月、物部氏の祖である[[伊香色雄]]（いかがしこお）に命じて、 {{quotation|物部（もののふ）の八十平瓮（やそびらか）を以て祭神之物（かみまつりもの）と作（な）さしむ<ref>『日本書紀』崇神天皇7年11月13日条</ref>}} とある。「平瓮」とは平らな土器。平たい皿様の器のことである。『古事記』中巻崇神天皇条にも同様の記載がある。
改新後も存続し、律令制下では[[囚獄司]]（40人）・[[衛門府]]（30人）・[[東西市司]]（各20人）に配属され、罪人の刑を執行する業務にあたった、という。
同様の職務を果たす部として、[[久米部]]がある。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p947、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p1132、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もののへ}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:部民]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|歴史用語|日本の古代氏族|物部氏|兵庫県津名郡にあった村|物部村 (兵庫県)|四国放送の女性アナウンサー|物部純子|その他の用法|物部 (曖昧さ回避)|}} '''物部'''（もののべ／もののふ）とは、古代[[日本]]の朝廷の兵（軍事）・刑（刑罰）に携わった職業部。令制諸官司にも配属されている[[品部]]。
== 概要 == 氏族的系譜を有し、[[大化の改新]]以前は[[物部氏|物部連]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきた。その数が多かったので、物部の八十氏などと呼ばれた。
『日本書紀』によると、崇神天皇7年の11月、物部氏の祖である[[伊香色雄]]（いかがしこお）に命じて、 {{quotation|物部（もののふ）の八十平瓮（やそびらか）を以て祭神之物（かみまつりもの）と作（な）さしむ<ref>『日本書紀』崇神天皇7年11月13日条</ref>}} とある。「平瓮」とは平らな土器。平たい皿様の器のことである。『古事記』中巻崇神天皇条にも同様の記載がある。
改新後も存続し、律令制下では[[囚獄司]]（40人）・[[衛門府]]（30人）・[[東西市司]]（各20人）に配属され、罪人の刑を執行する業務にあたった、という。
同様の職務を果たす部として、[[久米部]]がある。
== 物部氏 == 地方には物部連とは系統の異なる物部氏が多数存在した。 # [[笠原使主]]の子・兄麻呂を祖とする[[武蔵国造]]族の物部[[直 (カバネ)|直]]。 # [[飯入根命]]の子・宇乃遅命を祖とする[[出雲国造]]族の物部[[臣]]。 # 夏花命を祖とする[[上毛野国造]]同族の物部[[君 (カバネ)|君]]（[[磯部氏]]）。 # [[米餅搗大使主命]]の8世孫・額田臣と武蔵臣の兄弟を祖とする物部[[首 (姓)|首]]。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial..."
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:Be System]] [[Category:Japanese history]] [[ja:物部]]

== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.

The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:pp]][[category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pp]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.

== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.

== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.

The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] to [[Category:qqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

[[Category:Be System]] [[Category:Japanese history]] [[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]] [[category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:pp]][[category:Mononobe officials]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Mononobe officials]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:物部]]

{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.

During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 2]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}

{{Otheruses|Historical term|Ancient Japanese clan|{{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}|A village in Tsuna District, Hyōgo Prefecture|{{ill|Monobe Village, Hyōgo|ja|物部村 (兵庫県)}}|A female announcer at Shikoku Broadcasting|Junko Monobe|Other uses|{{ill|Monobe (disambiguation)|ja|物部 (曖昧さ回避)}}}} '''Mononobe''' (物部/もののべ/もののふ) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[品部|Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{afc comment|1=I think it is something like "manager of things" as the etymology but not sure and I do not have a source on that yet[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Mononobe clan}} '''Mononobe''' (物部) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Tier 2]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I think it is something like "manager of things" as the etymology but not sure and I do not have a source on that yet[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Mononobe clan}} '''Mononobe''' (物部) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首|12=draft}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命|12=draft}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟|12=draft}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三|12=draft}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二|12=draft}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I think it is something like "manager of things" as the etymology but not sure and I do not have a source on that yet[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Mononobe clan}} '''Mononobe''' (物部) refers to a professional group in ancient [[Japan]] involved in military and penal duties under the Imperial Court. It was part of the occupational categories known as [[Shinabe]], and members were also assigned to various Ritsuryō-era government offices.
== Overview == The Mononobe had genealogical ties and were historically overseen by the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}}, who bore the title of {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft}} before the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}}. Due to their large numbers, they were referred to as the "Eighty Mononobe Clans" (物部八十氏).
According to the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}*, in the seventh year of Emperor Sujin's reign, in the eleventh month, Mononobe's ancestor, {{ill|Ikagashiko-o|ja|伊香色雄|12=draft}}, was ordered: {{quotation|Let him craft the divine offerings using the eighty flat pots (平瓮, hirabira).<ref>*Nihon Shoki*, Sujin 7th year, 11th month, 13th day entry.</ref>}} "Flat pots" (平瓮, hirabira) refer to flat earthenware vessels resembling plates. A similar description can be found in the middle volume of the *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*, under the account of Emperor Sujin.
The Mononobe continued to exist after the reforms. During the Ritsuryō period, they were assigned to offices such as the {{ill|Shugokushi|ja|囚獄司|12=draft}} (40 people), {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} (30 people), and the {{ill|Tōzai no Ichishi|ja|東西市司|12=draft}} (20 people each). They were tasked with carrying out punishments on criminals.
Another occupational group with similar duties was the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部|12=draft}}.
== Mononobe Clan == Many Mononobe clans unrelated to the main {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}} existed in various regions: 1. The Mononobe {{ill|Naohi|ja|直|12=draft}}, descended from {{ill|Kasahara Sukune|ja|笠原使主|12=draft}}'s son, Ani-maro, part of the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造|12=draft}} lineage. 2. The Mononobe {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|12=draft}}, descended from {{ill|Iiinune no Mikoto|ja|飯入根命|12=draft}}'s son, Unoji-no-Mikoto, of the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=draft}} lineage. 3. The Mononobe {{ill|Kimi|ja|君|12=draft}}, descended from {{ill|Natsuhana no Mikoto|ja|夏花命|12=draft}}, part of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造|12=draft}} lineage (also associated with the {{ill|Isobe clan|ja|磯部氏|12=draft}}). 4. The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首|12=draft}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命|12=draft}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟|12=draft}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三|12=draft}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二|12=draft}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Be System]] [[Category:Japanese history]] [[Category:Mononobe officials]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mononobe officials]] [[ja:物部]] [[en:Mononobe officials]]

The Mononobe {{ill|Kashira|ja|首|12=draft}}, descended from Nukata-no-Omi and Musashi-no-Omi, both eighth-generation descendants of {{ill|Yonemochitsuki Taishujin no Mikoto|ja|米餅搗大使主命|12=draft}}.
== Notes ==

== References == * *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (Vol. 1), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 1)*, [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by {{ill|Takashi Ujitani|ja|宇治谷孟|12=draft}}, 1988. * *{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*: Complete Translation of Japan's Classics Vol. 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * *Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 947, edited by {{ill|Kōju Takayanagi|ja|高柳光寿|12=draft}} and {{ill|Risan Takeuchi|ja|竹内理三|12=draft}}, [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * *Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary*, p. 1132, supervised by {{ill|Keiji Nagahara|ja|永原慶二|12=draft}}, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
Text replacement - "|12=draft" to ""
Removing from [[Category:New]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
Text replacement - "|伊香色雄" to "|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o"
5 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:物部 up to rev 85675118
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|歴史用語|日本の古代氏族|物部氏|兵庫県津名郡にあった村|物部村 (兵庫県)|四国放送の女性アナウンサー|物部純子|その他の用法|物部 (曖昧さ回避)|}} '''物部'''（もののべ／もののふ）とは、古代[[日本]]の朝廷の兵（軍事）・刑（刑罰）に携わった職業部。令制諸官司にも配属されている[[品部]]。
== 概要 == 氏族的系譜を有し、[[大化の改新]]以前は[[物部氏|物部連]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきた。その数が多かったので、物部の八十氏などと呼ばれた。
『日本書紀』によると、崇神天皇7年の11月、物部氏の祖である[[伊香色雄]]（いかがしこお）に命じて、 {{quotation|物部（もののふ）の八十平瓮（やそびらか）を以て祭神之物（かみまつりもの）と作（な）さしむ<ref>『日本書紀』崇神天皇7年11月13日条</ref>}} とある。「平瓮」とは平らな土器。平たい皿様の器のことである。『古事記』中巻崇神天皇条にも同様の記載がある。
改新後も存続し、律令制下では[[囚獄司]]（40人）・[[衛門府]]（30人）・[[東西市司]]（各20人）に配属され、罪人の刑を執行する業務にあたった、という。
同様の職務を果たす部として、[[久米部]]がある。
== 物部氏 == 地方には物部連とは系統の異なる物部氏が多数存在した。 # [[笠原使主]]の子・兄麻呂を祖とする[[武蔵国造]]族の物部[[直 (カバネ)|直]]。 # [[飯入根命]]の子・宇乃遅命を祖とする[[出雲国造]]族の物部[[臣]]。 # 夏花命を祖とする[[上毛野国造]]同族の物部[[君 (カバネ)|君]]（[[磯部氏]]）。 # [[米餅搗大使主命]]の8世孫・額田臣と武蔵臣の兄弟を祖とする物部[[首 (姓)|首]]。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p947、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p1132、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もののへ}} [[Category:部民]]

== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
